The main experiments are conducted primarily on CIFAR-10, with additional evaluations reported in the original work on larger-scale settings such as ImageNet and WMT16, to support the claim that learning rate rewinding is an effective and general retraining strategy after pruning.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is methodologically wrong to prune only after full dense training instead of pruning during training or from initialization.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This paper is explicitly evaluating post-pruning recovery strategies after standard magnitude pruning of a trained model. Pruning after dense training is not a mistake here; it is the controlled setup needed to isolate whether fine-tuning, weight rewinding, or learning-rate rewinding best restores performance once a trained network has been sparsified.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Learning rate rewinding is invalid because rewinding only the optimizer schedule without rewinding the weights creates a mismatched state.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: That apparent mismatch is the core hypothesis being tested, not a protocol error. The proposed method intentionally keeps the final trained weights while replaying an earlier portion of the learning-rate schedule to test whether the recovery benefit comes mainly from the schedule rather than from resetting weights. As long as this is compared consistently against fine-tuning and weight rewinding, it is a sound experimental design.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Weight rewinding is unfair or incorrect because it resets only surviving weights to earlier values after pruning, instead of retraining the whole dense model from that checkpoint.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The goal is to evaluate how a fixed pruned subnetwork recovers under different post-pruning retraining procedures. Resetting the surviving weights to their earlier values is exactly how weight rewinding is defined in this literature, and it keeps the sparse architecture fixed so the comparison focuses on retraining strategy rather than changing which parameters are present.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using CIFAR-10 as the main experimental testbed is too small to support conclusions about pruning retraining methods.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For a reproducibility study comparing post-pruning recovery strategies across many sparsity levels, CIFAR-10 is a standard and practical benchmark that enables careful controlled comparisons at reasonable cost. The claim of broader relevance is additionally supported by referencing the original paper's larger-scale ImageNet and WMT16 results; using CIFAR-10 for the main reproduction is therefore not itself a flawed design choice.

title: Re] Exacerbating Algorithmic Bias through Fairness Attacks
task_prompt:
The paper studies fairness-targeted data poisoning attacks in a binary classification setting with two demographic groups, an advantaged group and a disadvantaged group. Starting from a clean training set, the adversary constructs a poisoned set and appends it to the original training data so that a model trained on the combined dataset becomes less fair. The evaluation is framed around common fairness metrics for two-group settings, and the attacks are compared against prior poisoning approaches from the literature.

The first proposed method, the influence attack on fairness, generates adversarial training points by optimizing an adversarial objective that increases when the learned model makes more unfair decisions. The attack uses gradient-based optimization to search for poison points that maximize this fairness-related loss, and includes a regularization parameter to control the trade-off between degrading fairness and degrading predictive performance.

The second proposed method, the anchoring attack, creates poison points near selected target examples from the two demographic groups. Specifically, it uses one target point from the advantaged group and one from the disadvantaged group, and places nearby poisoned samples with opposite class labels while preserving the demographic attribute of the corresponding target. The paper evaluates whether these attacks can worsen fairness, whether the anchoring attack can do so while leaving accuracy largely unchanged, and whether the influence-based attack provides a controllable fairness-performance trade-off.

flaw_records.description: The paper omits critical technical details needed to implement the proposed attacks, including information required for the algorithms themselves and key parts of the experimental setup. The missing specification forced reconstruction of the intended setup from the released code and related literature rather than from the paper alone.

The model definitions, training pipeline, hyperparameter choices, and data preprocessing procedures are not described in sufficient detail. Because these components materially affect reported attack behavior, the lack of explicit settings makes exact reproduction of the experimental methodology difficult and leaves room for divergent implementations.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using fairness degradation rather than accuracy degradation as the primary attack objective is misguided, because poisoning attacks should only be evaluated by drops in predictive performance.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This paper's hypothesis is specifically that an adversary can worsen group fairness even when conventional utility metrics do not show a large collapse. In fairness-oriented security settings, fairness is the targeted quantity, not merely a side effect of lower accuracy. Evaluating attacks against fairness metrics in a two-group binary classification setup is therefore a domain-appropriate objective, not a methodological mistake.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Appending poisoned points to the clean training set is an unrealistic or incorrect threat model; the attacker should replace or modify existing training data instead.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Data poisoning is commonly modeled as injecting additional malicious samples into an otherwise clean training pipeline, especially when the attacker can submit data but cannot directly edit trusted records. Studying appended poison points is therefore a standard and meaningful attack model. It is also appropriate here because the paper is testing whether fairness can be manipulated through plausible training-data injection.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Including a regularization parameter to trade off fairness degradation against predictive performance shows the attack is improperly constrained or not a true fairness attack.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The trade-off parameter is a principled design choice because the paper explicitly studies whether an attacker can worsen fairness while preserving much of the model's utility. In adversarial settings, controllability is valuable: some attackers may want maximal unfairness regardless of accuracy loss, while others prefer stealthier attacks with limited performance impact. A tunable objective directly supports this hypothesis rather than undermining it.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The anchoring attack is invalid because it intentionally flips class labels near selected target points while keeping the demographic attribute fixed, which looks like unrealistic label noise rather than a legitimate poisoning strategy.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For clean-label realism this might be questionable, but as a general poisoning design it is coherent: the method is constructing adversarially chosen training examples to distort the learned decision boundary in a group-dependent way. Preserving the demographic attribute while assigning the opposite class label near group-specific anchor points is exactly what makes the attack target fairness disparities between groups. That unusual construction is aligned with the paper's mechanism, not an inherent methodological error.

mixed_rationale: The main experimental claims were largely reproduced in trend, but the methodology was insufficiently specified in several implementation-critical areas.

title: Re] Replication study of 'Data-Driven Methods for Balancing Fairness and Efficiency in Ride-Pooling
task_prompt:
The paper studies how different optimization objectives in ride-pooling affect efficiency and fairness for both riders and drivers. It evaluates four objective functions within a dispatching framework based on a Markov decision process combined with a neural value estimator, following a non-myopic matching approach that considers future consequences of current assignments. The system operates on a strongly connected street-network graph with precomputed routes and travel times between all node pairs, and uses trip-request data in which each request has an origin, destination, and request time.

The methodology compares two profitability-oriented objectives and two fairness-oriented objectives. The profitability objectives are: maximizing the total number of serviced requests, and maximizing total driver income. The rider-fairness objective seeks to maintain profitability while reducing disparities in service rates across neighborhoods, and the driver-fairness objective seeks to reduce inequality in income across drivers while still accounting for system performance. For each objective, the dispatching algorithm is run in simulation on New York Yellow Taxi request data to produce ride matches over time.

The paper evaluates each objective using three main outcomes: overall success rate, defined as the percentage of all requests that are serviced; success rate in the worst-served neighborhood, used as a measure of rider fairness; and the distribution of income across drivers, including attention to low-earning drivers as a measure of driver fairness. The overall success rate is treated as a proxy for company profitability. The paper analyzes the trade-offs among these metrics across the four objectives, including whether fairness-oriented objectives can improve worst-neighborhood service or driver income equity without sacrificing overall system efficiency.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using overall success rate as a proxy for company profitability is invalid because profitability must be measured with explicit revenue and cost accounting.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In this ride-pooling setting, the paper is comparing dispatch objectives under a fixed simulation environment rather than estimating literal firm balance sheets. Served-request rate is a standard operational proxy for platform-side efficiency and revenue opportunity when request demand is exogenous and the main question is relative trade-offs among objectives. It is therefore a defensible evaluation metric for comparing objectives, even if it is not a full accounting profit measure.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating rider fairness via the success rate of the worst-served neighborhood is too narrow and therefore an incorrect fairness metric.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is an intentionally stringent group-fairness criterion, not a mistake. For spatial service systems, the minimum neighborhood service rate directly captures whether any area is being systematically left behind, which is exactly the disparity the rider-fairness objective is designed to address. A reviewer might prefer average disparity metrics, but focusing on the worst-served neighborhood is a legitimate domain-specific choice when the hypothesis concerns geographic under-service.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using a non-myopic MDP with a neural value estimator for dispatch is unnecessarily risky; the study should use greedy one-step matching to make objective comparisons valid.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Ride-pooling dispatch is inherently sequential: assigning a driver now changes future vehicle availability, pooling opportunities, and service outcomes. A non-myopic formulation is therefore well matched to the domain, especially when the paper's goal is to study long-run fairness-efficiency trade-offs rather than only instantaneous matching quality. Using the same dispatch framework across all four objectives supports fair comparison; the choice is unusual only relative to simpler baselines, not incorrect.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Precomputing routes and travel times between all node pairs on a strongly connected street-network graph is an unrealistic simplification that invalidates the dispatch simulation.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For large-scale ride-matching simulations, precomputing shortest-path routes and travel times is a standard engineering choice that makes repeated assignment evaluation tractable. Strong connectivity is also a reasonable assumption for an urban road network representation because the dispatch algorithm needs feasible travel between nodes. Unless the paper claims dynamic traffic estimation, this simplification is compatible with the study's objective-comparison focus and does not by itself undermine validity.

title: Re] Replicating and Improving GAN2Shape Through Novel Shape Priors and Training Steps
task_prompt:
The original GAN2Shape paper presents an unsupervised framework for recovering 3D information from images by leveraging a pre-trained StyleGAN2 generator. The central idea is that a GAN trained on a category-specific image collection implicitly stores information about object geometry, viewpoint, and illumination in its weights and latent space. Given an input image from the same category as the pre-trained GAN, the method first projects or inverts the image into the StyleGAN2 latent space to obtain a latent code whose generated image matches the input.

From this latent representation, the framework extracts 3D shape by coupling the GAN image prior with feature-based reconstruction. The method uses pre-trained feature extraction networks and optimization-based inference to estimate scene properties such as depth or shape, albedo/texture, lighting, and viewpoint for the dominant object in the image. The recovered 3D representation is intended to arise without requiring annotations, keypoints, multi-view supervision, or explicit 3D ground-truth labels, and is described as applicable across several object categories including faces, cats, cars, and buildings.

Once the 3D shape and appearance factors are inferred, the reconstructed object can be rendered under altered conditions. In particular, the claimed output supports geometric manipulation such as changing viewpoint and photometric manipulation such as re-lighting, allowing the original 2D image to be edited using the recovered 3D scene structure. The method is framed as exploiting intrinsic knowledge already present in a standard 2D GAN, avoiding changes to GAN training and avoiding supervised 3D training pipelines.

flaw_records.description: Key implementation details needed to reproduce the method were not properly specified in the paper, especially the exact loss functions and the number of training steps. These had to be inferred from the released code rather than from the publication itself, indicating that the methodological description was incomplete and insufficient for an independent re-implementation from the paper alone.

Some parts of the method described in the paper did not match the actual implementation used by the original authors, particularly the loss functions. This discrepancy means the published methodology and the operative code were not fully aligned, making the intended training objective ambiguous and contestable.

The paper claims high-precision 3D shape recovery across multiple categories and broad applicability without annotations or assumptions, but the replication obtained only varying degrees of success on a subset of categories and explicitly observed both successes and shortcomings. This challenges the breadth and strength of the original performance claims.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using a pre-trained 2D StyleGAN2 as the core prior for 3D reconstruction is conceptually invalid because a network trained only on images cannot contain usable geometric information.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is precisely the paper's hypothesis and is a reasonable one for category-specific generators: to synthesize realistic images across pose, lighting, and instance variation, the GAN often organizes latent factors in ways that correlate with underlying 3D structure. Exploiting that implicit prior is unusual but well-motivated, and does not require the GAN to have been explicitly trained with 3D supervision.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Projecting each input image into the GAN latent space before estimating shape is an unnecessary or biased detour; reconstruction should be done directly from pixels with a separately trained 3D model.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For this method, inversion is the mechanism that ties the observed image to the category-specific image prior stored in the GAN. It constrains inference to the generator's learned manifold, which is exactly how the approach regularizes an otherwise ill-posed single-image 3D problem. This is a deliberate design choice, not a methodological mistake.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Relying on pre-trained feature extractors and optimization-based inference instead of end-to-end supervised training means the method is incomplete or improperly trained.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In unsupervised single-image 3D recovery, feature-based perceptual objectives and test-time optimization are standard and often necessary because there is no direct 3D supervision. The goal here is to recover shape, appearance, lighting, and viewpoint from an image using fixed priors rather than to train a new fully supervised predictor. That makes optimization-based inference an appropriate choice.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Restricting the method to category-specific GANs and same-category inputs is a weakness that undermines the approach; a correct method should be category-agnostic.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The use of category-specific generators is central to the method's rationale: strong within-category regularities make it plausible that the GAN has encoded consistent pose, appearance, and geometric structure. For single-image 3D reconstruction without labels, this narrower setting is a justified tradeoff that strengthens the prior rather than indicating a flaw.

mixed_rationale: The report supports that the method can work in some settings, but documents major methodological underspecification and overbroad claims about precision and general applicability.

title: Re] Value Alignment Verification
task_prompt:
The paper studies value alignment verification: determining whether a robot's behavior is aligned with a human's preferences while using as few test queries as possible. The methodology is framed around constructing a minimal questionnaire that can certify alignment between agents. The paper considers multiple verification settings of increasing difficulty, including cases where both agents' reward functions are explicit and cases where the robot's reward is implicit and must be inferred from its action preferences or trajectory preferences.

Methodologically, the paper proposes algorithms and heuristics for exact and approximate value alignment verification. In the explicit-human, explicit-robot setting, the human's reward function is used to generate diagnostic tests that distinguish aligned from misaligned behaviors. In the explicit-human, implicit-robot setting, the verifier instead queries the robot about action preferences in selected states. More generally, the framework aims to choose a compact set of informative comparisons or questions that can efficiently verify whether the robot is behaviorally compatible with the human.

To validate the approach, the paper evaluates the proposed verification procedures across a range of environments, including several gridworld domains and a continuous autonomous driving domain. The empirical evaluation is designed to measure whether the proposed algorithms and heuristics can achieve high verification accuracy with low false positive and false negative rates while minimizing the number of questions required. The overall experimental claim is that efficient questionnaires can reliably verify alignment across different settings and domains.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The paper is flawed because it evaluates verification via questionnaires or selected preference queries rather than by running the robot extensively and measuring full task performance.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's target problem is verification under query budget, not policy optimization or downstream task benchmarking. If the goal is to certify alignment with as few tests as possible, then constructing diagnostic questions is the correct object of study. Extensive rollout-based evaluation would answer a different question and would obscure whether the proposed method actually reduces the amount of information needed for verification.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is methodologically inconsistent to study multiple settings where the robot reward is sometimes explicit and sometimes implicit; the paper should choose one representation and evaluate only there.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: These settings correspond to genuinely different observability regimes in alignment verification. In some applications the verifier has direct access to both reward functions, while in others the robot's objectives are only inferable through action or trajectory preferences. Evaluating across this ladder of difficulty is a sound design choice because it tests whether the framework generalizes as access to the robot's internals becomes more limited.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using the human's explicit reward to generate diagnostic tests is unrealistic and therefore invalidates the explicit-human experiments.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The explicit-human setting is a legitimate and informative verification regime, not an unrealistic mistake. Many alignment formulations assume a known specification, proxy reward, or oracle preference model for the human side, and the point here is to isolate the verification problem once the reference values are available. Including this setting provides a clean baseline and allows the paper to separate difficulty caused by verification from difficulty caused by learning the human's preferences.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The experiments are suspect because they mix small gridworlds with a continuous autonomous driving domain instead of focusing on one standard benchmark family.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For a verification paper, this mixture is well motivated: gridworlds allow exact or near-exact analysis of questionnaire construction and error behavior, while a continuous driving domain checks that the ideas are not confined to toy discrete environments. The combination is a standard way to test both mechanistic correctness and practical portability of a verification framework.

title: Re] Replication Study of "Fairness and Bias in Online Selection
task_prompt:
The paper studies fairness in online selection problems and proposes fair alternatives for two classic settings: the Multi-Color Secretary problem and the Multi-Color Prophet problem. In these settings, candidates arrive sequentially and decisions must be made online. The paper defines fairness with respect to a sensitive group attribute by requiring that the probability of selecting a group matches the prior probability p that the best candidate comes from that group. The empirical goals are to compare the proposed fair algorithms against existing baselines on both fairness and performance.

For the Secretary setting, the paper presents one fair multi-color secretary algorithm and evaluates it against baseline secretary methods. Performance is measured as the probability of selecting the optimal candidate. For the Prophet setting, the paper presents two fair multi-color prophet algorithms and compares them with baseline prophet methods. Performance there is measured by the average value of the selected candidate. Across both problems, the paper uses a mix of synthetic and real-data experiments under multiple parameter settings.

The claimed experimental methodology is to run all algorithms on the same datasets and compare them using fairness and utility metrics. The baselines are included to represent existing approaches that may be either unfair or less competitive in performance, while the proposed methods are intended to be perfectly fair and still achieve performance comparable to or better than the baselines. The paper provides algorithm descriptions and pseudocode for the proposed methods, and the original experiments were implemented in C++.

flaw_records.description: One baseline in the second Multi-Color Secretary experiment produced results that differed because of inconsistencies in the original implementation. This means at least one reported baseline comparison relied on a baseline execution that was not implemented consistently with the paper's experimental setup.

The Multi-Color Prophet experiments were run with twice as many repetitions as the paper reported. As a result, the published description of the experimental sampling budget did not match the actual evaluation procedure used to generate the reported results, affecting exact reproducibility until the run count was corrected.

The report documents multiple inconsistencies between the paper and the released code, along with missing implementation parts in the code base. These discrepancies made the experimental procedure underspecified from the paper-plus-code artifacts alone and required clarification beyond the published materials.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using different utility metrics across the two problems is invalid; the paper should evaluate both Secretary and Prophet algorithms with the same performance measure.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The two online selection settings have different canonical objectives. In Secretary problems the standard goal is probability of selecting the single best candidate, while in Prophet problems the standard goal is expected reward/value of the accepted item. Matching each experiment to the accepted objective for that problem is methodologically appropriate, not a flaw.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Defining fairness as matching each group's selection probability to the prior probability that the best candidate comes from that group is an arbitrary or wrong fairness target; fairness should instead force equal selection rates across groups.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In online selection with heterogeneous group priors, equal selection rates can be misaligned with merit-based opportunity. The paper's fairness notion is tailored to this domain: it calibrates selection probability to the ex ante chance that the top candidate belongs to each group, which is exactly the fairness criterion the algorithms are designed to satisfy.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing the proposed fair methods against baselines that are known to be unfair is not a meaningful evaluation because the baselines do not optimize the same fairness objective.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The stated empirical question is precisely about the fairness-utility tradeoff relative to existing online selection methods. Including standard baselines, even if they are unfair, is necessary to show whether the proposed algorithms improve fairness while remaining competitive in utility. A baseline need not satisfy the new fairness constraint to be an appropriate reference.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using both synthetic and real-data experiments under multiple parameter settings is suspicious because the paper should pick one evaluation regime for consistency.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For online selection and fairness studies, synthetic data is useful for controlled stress tests where priors and arrival/value distributions can be varied systematically, while real data checks external relevance. Using both is a sound design choice because they answer complementary questions about robustness and practical behavior.

mixed_rationale: Most reported claims were reproducible after correcting implementation/reporting inconsistencies, but specific experimental results and one baseline outcome were affected by those issues.

title: ¬Re] Reproducing 'Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a fair selective classification framework based on sufficiency. In this setting, a classifier is allowed to abstain from making predictions, typically by applying a confidence threshold so that only sufficiently confident predictions are retained. The claimed goal is to ensure that selective prediction does not exacerbate disparities between sensitive groups as coverage changes. To do this, the method introduces a sufficiency criterion intended to align predicted scores with outcomes across groups, together with a regularization term based on mutual information.

The methodology compares a sufficiency-regularized selective classifier against a standard selective classification baseline. The paper evaluates whether the proposed method reduces disparities in precision across the full coverage scale and in margin distributions, while preserving fairness when examining individual classification scores. The authors also note that using sufficiency may reduce overall accuracy in some settings, but claim that it still improves fairness by decreasing group disparities.

The approach is demonstrated on four datasets spanning different data modalities, including traditional fairness benchmark datasets. Evaluation focuses on fairness and performance under selective classification, including accuracy, precision across coverage levels, and group-wise margin behavior. The paper claims that the sufficiency-regularized classifier achieves better fairness performance than the baseline and that it mitigates disparities without increasing them.

flaw_records.description: Many technical details and model hyperparameters needed to implement the method were not specified in the paper, requiring substantial experimentation to fill in the missing choices. The report states that clarification had to be requested from the authors and that some of this information only arrived very late, indicating that the published methodology did not fully define the training setup.

Some figures and presentation choices in the paper were ambiguous enough to create confusion about the exact implementation of the model. This made the intended procedure difficult to reconstruct from the paper alone and contributed to uncertainty about whether the reimplementation matched the original methodology.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using sufficiency rather than parity-style fairness constraints is the wrong fairness definition for selective classification and therefore a methodological mistake.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This paper's hypothesis is specifically about preventing selective prediction from distorting how scores relate to outcomes across groups as coverage changes. Sufficiency is directly relevant to that objective because it concerns calibration-like alignment between predicted scores and true outcomes conditional on the score, which is precisely the quantity that threshold-based abstention uses. A parity metric would answer a different fairness question; choosing sufficiency here is a domain-matched design choice, not an error.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Adding a mutual-information regularizer to enforce sufficiency is ad hoc or unjustified because fairness should be measured only with explicit group metrics at evaluation time.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: A regularizer based on mutual information is a reasonable training-time mechanism for discouraging residual dependence between sensitive-group membership and prediction behavior beyond what should remain under sufficiency. In fairness work it is common to optimize a surrogate objective that encourages the desired conditional independence or alignment property, then evaluate with downstream fairness metrics. The regularizer is therefore a principled proxy, not an arbitrary add-on.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating precision disparities over the full coverage scale and inspecting group-wise margin distributions is improper; the paper should report fairness only at a single operating threshold or with standard aggregate metrics.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Selective classification is fundamentally threshold-dependent because abstention changes which examples are retained. A method can look fair at one threshold yet induce disparities at other coverage levels. Examining fairness across the full coverage curve and via margin distributions is therefore especially appropriate in this setting, since it tests whether disparities emerge as the abstention threshold moves. This is more informative than a single-threshold snapshot.

must_not_claim_details.claim: A fairness method that lowers overall accuracy in some settings is invalid, so any reported fairness gain should be dismissed as an artifact.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In fair machine learning, fairness-performance tradeoffs are common and often unavoidable. The paper explicitly frames its contribution as reducing group disparities under selective classification, not universally improving both fairness and accuracy. If the method preserves the intended score-level fairness properties and improves disparity metrics while making the accuracy tradeoff transparent, that is a legitimate and expected design outcome rather than evidence of methodological failure.

mixed_rationale: The report supports the high-level fairness trend claimed for sufficiency, but documents that missing methodological details and ambiguous implementation information make the original experimental design contestable and difficult to verify.

title: Re] Differentiable Spatial Planning using Transformers
task_prompt:
The paper studies differentiable spatial path planning and proposes a Spatial Planning Transformer (SPT) architecture for navigation on map-like environments. The claimed methodology evaluates whether transformer-based planning can replace prior CNN-based iterative planning modules that propagate information locally. The planner is trained to predict navigation actions from spatial representations and is compared against established data-driven planning baselines such as convolutional planning networks.

The experiments are described on synthetic map domains with varying obstacle layouts and complexity levels. The paper claims to measure average action prediction accuracy for the planner while varying map and goal configurations, and to test whether the SPT model maintains stronger performance than prior methods as obstacle complexity increases. A central part of the methodology is to benchmark SPT against CNN-based planning baselines under matched planning settings to quantify gains in planning accuracy.

Beyond standalone planning, the paper also presents an end-to-end differentiable mapping-and-planning setup. In this setting, the transformer-based planning module is combined with a learned mapper so that the system can learn navigation without requiring a ground-truth map at inference time. The claimed experimental design compares this end-to-end mapper-plus-planner system against classical mapping and planning baselines, with emphasis on generalization to out-of-distribution maps and goals as well as the ability to learn mapping and planning jointly within a differentiable framework.

flaw_records.description: The paper's action prediction accuracy metric is described too vaguely to uniquely determine how evaluation should be implemented. This ambiguity leaves multiple plausible interpretations of the metric and makes direct comparison to the reported accuracy difficult to verify.

The paper claims that the Spatial Planning Transformer definitively outperforms prior CNN-based baselines, but the reproduced results described in the report only show superiority in select cases rather than consistently across the benchmark settings. This challenges the breadth of the paper's stated conclusion relative to the observed evidence.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using synthetic map domains instead of real-world navigation environments makes the planning evaluation invalid or too unrealistic to support conclusions.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For this paper's hypothesis, synthetic grid/map domains are an appropriate controlled testbed because they isolate the planning computation itself from confounds such as perception noise, simulator dynamics, and sensor artifacts. Since the goal is to compare a transformer planner against CNN-based planning modules under matched spatial planning settings, synthetic obstacle layouts are a sound way to vary path complexity systematically and measure whether the architecture improves planning.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating the planner by predicting navigation actions rather than full trajectories or path cost is the wrong objective for a spatial planning model.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In differentiable planning literature, action prediction from spatial state/goal representations is a standard supervised proxy for whether the model has learned the underlying planning policy. It aligns directly with prior learned planning baselines and allows controlled comparison of decision quality at each state. Although other metrics could also be informative, using action prediction accuracy is not inherently a methodological error for benchmarking learned planners.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Benchmarking the transformer planner primarily against CNN-based iterative planning modules is an unfair or outdated comparison because the baselines are architecturally different.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: That architectural difference is exactly the point of the paper's research question: whether transformer-based spatial planning can replace prior CNN-style local propagation planners. Comparing against established convolutional planning networks under matched planning settings is therefore the correct ablation target, not a flaw. A method intended as a drop-in alternative should be judged against the dominant prior family it seeks to improve upon.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Combining a learned mapper and planner in one end-to-end differentiable system is unsound because mapping and planning should be evaluated separately and learning without a ground-truth map at inference is inappropriate.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: An end-to-end mapper-plus-planner setup is justified when the claim is that differentiability enables joint optimization of representation building and planning for navigation. Requiring a ground-truth map only during training or supervision but not at inference is a realistic and meaningful design choice, since deployed agents typically must navigate from observations alone. Evaluating both standalone planning and joint mapping-and-planning is therefore methodologically coherent rather than incorrect.

mixed_rationale: The report questions the clarity of the reported evaluation metric and the strength of the paper's superiority claims, while still indicating that parts of the planner methodology were largely reproducible.

title: Re] Solving Phase Retrieval With a Learned Reference
task_prompt:
The paper studies phase retrieval from Fourier magnitude measurements in a setting where a reference image is added to the unknown signal before measurement. The central idea is to optimize this reference so that reconstruction becomes easier and more accurate. To do this, the method formulates phase retrieval as an iterative algorithm and represents the iterations as an unrolled neural network whose parameters include a learnable reference.

Training is performed using a small amount of data from a target distribution to learn a reference that improves recovery of signals from that distribution. At test time, the learned reference is used within the iterative reconstruction procedure to recover previously unseen signals from their Fourier magnitudes. The paper presents this learned-reference approach as a practical way to incorporate data-driven structure into a classical inverse problem while keeping the reconstruction process algorithmic and interpretable through the unrolled design.

The experimental methodology evaluates whether the learned reference improves reconstruction quality and whether it generalizes beyond the specific training examples. In particular, the paper reports that references learned from limited training data generalize well to unseen data distributions. It also tests robustness under spatial transformations such as shifts and rotations, arguing that the learned reference remains effective under these augmentations.

flaw_records.description: The methodology did not provide enough information about the learning rates used in both the training procedure and the retrieval process, even though these settings materially influence performance and required separate investigation before results could be matched effectively.

The available implementation was only marginally documented, and computational tricks used by the method were not explained in detail, reducing transparency of how the claimed procedure should be executed and making independent verification more difficult.

The released code contained redundant components that slowed computation, indicating that the practical methodology as provided was not cleanly specified for efficient reproduction and required refactoring before extensive experiments could be run.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using an unrolled iterative phase-retrieval network with a learnable physical reference is an unjustified hybrid that sacrifices correctness compared with either a fully model-based solver or a standard end-to-end neural network.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This hybridization is exactly the methodological point: phase retrieval here is a structured inverse problem with known measurement physics, and unrolling preserves the algorithmic reconstruction steps while exposing a small set of trainable components tied to that physics. Learning the reference through the unrolled solver is domain-appropriate because the reference changes the measurement conditioning itself, not just the post-processing, so embedding it inside the iterative reconstruction is a principled way to optimize for recovery performance while retaining interpretability.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Learning the reference from only a small amount of target-distribution data is methodologically unsound and almost certainly overfits, so claims about improved recovery should be dismissed unless trained on a large generic dataset.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The goal is not broad semantic representation learning but adaptation of a low-dimensional measurement design element—the reference—to a target signal family. In this setting, a small amount of representative data can be sufficient to tune measurement conditioning toward that distribution. Because the learned object is a shared reference used for all future reconstructions rather than a per-example predictor, the sample complexity is much lower than for standard image-to-image deep learning, making limited-data training a defensible and hypothesis-aligned choice.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating whether a learned reference generalizes to unseen or even shifted/rotated data distributions is inappropriate because a reference optimized on one training set should only be expected to work on those exact examples.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Generalization is a central scientific question for this method, not a methodological mistake. The paper's claim is that the learned reference captures structural properties of a signal class that improve phase retrieval beyond memorized training samples. Testing on unseen samples and under spatial transformations is therefore the correct way to probe whether the learned reference improves measurement geometry in a distribution-level sense rather than merely fitting the training set.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Testing robustness under shifts and rotations is misguided because Fourier-magnitude phase retrieval is too sensitive to spatial transforms for such augmentation-based evaluation to be meaningful.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Spatial transforms are especially relevant in Fourier-based imaging problems because they induce structured, well-understood changes in the signal and can expose whether the learned reference is tied to brittle pixel alignment or to more intrinsic recovery properties. Including shifts and rotations is therefore a justified stress test of invariance/robustness for a learned measurement design, not an invalid evaluation choice.

mixed_rationale: The reproduction largely supports the paper's main claims, but it documents that key implementation and hyperparameter details were insufficiently specified, making the methodology harder to verify and tune independently.

title: Re] Reproducibility Report: Contrastive Learning of Socially-aware Motion Representations
task_prompt:
The paper proposes Social-NCE, a socially aware contrastive loss for multi-agent motion prediction and navigation. The core idea is that standard trajectory datasets mostly contain safe behavior and therefore do not provide explicit negative examples of socially unacceptable or colliding futures. To address this, the method augments existing models with a contrastive objective that uses ground-truth information to construct positive and negative samples, encouraging learned motion representations to separate socially valid trajectories from collision-prone ones.

The claimed methodology applies this Social-NCE loss on top of several existing trajectory forecasting architectures, including human trajectory prediction models such as Social-STGCNN and Trajectron++, as well as models for robot navigation. The paper evaluates whether adding the loss reduces collision rates while preserving predictive accuracy, especially final displacement error, relative to the underlying base models. The approach is intended to improve robustness without requiring an entirely new forecasting architecture.

Beyond human trajectory forecasting, the paper also extends the same socially contrastive training idea to robot navigation settings. In imitation learning, the method is used to reduce collisions in crowded environments. In reinforcement learning, the claim is that incorporating socially aware contrastive learning improves sample efficiency and helps learn collision-free policies more quickly. The overall experimental design is therefore a multi-benchmark comparison in which baseline models are trained with and without Social-NCE and then assessed on collision-related and prediction-performance metrics.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using ground-truth future information to construct positive and negative samples for the contrastive loss is label leakage and makes the method invalid for forecasting.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is a training-time supervision design, not a test-time input. In trajectory forecasting it is standard to use future ground truth during training to define losses or supervision signals, while inference must operate without it. Here the future labels are used only to shape the representation so that socially valid and collision-prone futures are separated; that does not imply the model is given forbidden information at deployment.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Adding a socially aware contrastive loss on top of existing forecasting and navigation models is methodologically unsound because the paper should instead build a new architecture specialized for social interaction.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis being tested is precisely that social acceptability can be improved as a training objective independent of backbone architecture. Treating Social-NCE as a plug-in loss is a valid and actually stronger design choice for this claim, because it checks whether the effect generalizes across different model families rather than depending on one custom architecture.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating collision reduction while also checking that final displacement error is preserved is internally inconsistent, because optimizing social safety rather than pure prediction accuracy should make accuracy metrics irrelevant.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In this domain there is a real tradeoff concern: a method could trivially avoid collisions by producing overly conservative or inaccurate trajectories. Jointly reporting collision metrics and standard forecasting metrics such as FDE is therefore the correct evaluation design for a safety-aware forecasting loss, since it tests whether safety improves without degrading predictive fidelity.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Applying the same socially contrastive idea across supervised trajectory prediction, imitation learning, and reinforcement learning is too heterogeneous to be a meaningful experiment and suggests the method is misapplied outside its original setting.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The unifying object across these settings is learned motion representation under social constraints, not a single task formulation. Social interaction violations such as collisions are relevant in forecasting and navigation alike, so testing the same auxiliary objective across IL and RL is a legitimate way to assess whether the representation-level inductive bias transfers across learning paradigms.

title: Re] From goals, waypoints and paths to longterm human trajectory forecasting
task_prompt:
The paper proposes YNet, a multi-modal trajectory forecasting model for long-term human path prediction. The method represents future motion as influenced by both intentional goal selection and uncertainty in how a path is executed. To capture this, the model first predicts a probability distribution over possible future destinations and intermediate waypoints, corresponding to the broad spatial intent of an agent, and then predicts full future paths conditioned on those sampled goals and waypoints.

The architecture combines scene context and motion history by using semantic segmentation maps together with past trajectory heatmaps as input. These inputs are processed by a set of U-Net-style subnetworks that produce probabilistic heatmaps for future goal locations, waypoints, and trajectory points. The model is designed to support multi-modal prediction by sampling multiple plausible future endpoints and intermediate points from these predicted distributions.

In the claimed inference procedure, the model first estimates where an agent may go, then samples future goal and waypoint candidates, and finally generates distributions over the remaining trajectory conditioned on those samples. This two-stage decomposition is intended to separately model high-level destination uncertainty and lower-level path variability. The paper evaluates this framework on pedestrian trajectory forecasting benchmarks such as SDD, ETH/UCY, and InD, claiming strong short-term and long-term prediction performance relative to prior methods.

flaw_records.description: The experimental methodology for ETH/UCY could not be directly verified because the code and data required to reproduce those experiments were missing from the released materials. The report states that reproducing these results required proceeding from an independent interpretation of the paper and code rather than from a complete author-provided pipeline.

The claim that results were broadly reproducible across datasets is weakened by substantial divergence on long-term prediction results for the InD dataset. While several benchmarks were matched closely, the report notes that this specific setting produced materially different outcomes, indicating that the strength of the paper's dataset-wide performance claims is contestable.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Decomposing prediction into first sampling goals/waypoints and then predicting trajectories is an unjustified, error-prone two-stage pipeline that should be replaced by direct end-to-end trajectory prediction.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For long-horizon human forecasting, destination uncertainty and path-execution uncertainty are qualitatively different sources of multimodality. Separating high-level intent (goal/waypoint selection) from low-level motion generation is a domain-appropriate inductive bias, not a flaw. A direct one-shot predictor often averages across incompatible futures, while the staged design is specifically intended to preserve multiple plausible long-term outcomes.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Predicting future positions as heatmaps over scene space instead of regressing coordinates is a poor or indirect formulation for trajectory forecasting.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Heatmaps are well motivated here because the output is inherently multimodal and spatially constrained by scene layout. Representing destinations, waypoints, and paths as probability maps allows multiple plausible modes to coexist and aligns naturally with semantic map inputs. In this domain, coordinate regression can be less suitable because it tends to collapse uncertainty into averaged, physically implausible trajectories.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using semantic segmentation maps together with past trajectory heatmaps as image-like inputs to U-Net subnetworks is an odd misuse of a vision architecture for sequential motion forecasting.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is justified because pedestrian forecasting is strongly conditioned by spatial scene structure such as sidewalks, roads, obstacles, and crossing areas. Casting both scene context and motion history into a common top-down spatial representation lets convolutional encoder-decoder networks exploit local and global spatial regularities effectively. The choice differs from pure sequence models but is appropriate for map-conditioned trajectory prediction.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Sampling multiple candidate endpoints and intermediate waypoints at inference is ad hoc randomness and makes the method unreliable compared with outputting a single deterministic forecast.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In multi-modal trajectory forecasting, sampling is the standard mechanism for representing several plausible futures rather than pretending there is only one. Human motion, especially over long horizons, is not deterministic from past observations alone. Sampling from predicted goal and waypoint distributions is therefore a principled way to cover diverse valid futures and is consistent with the paper's modeling hypothesis.

mixed_rationale: The report largely supports the paper's core methodology and many results, but contests the completeness and reproducibility of some dataset-specific experimental support.

title: Re] Graph Edit Networks
task_prompt:
The paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a graph neural network architecture designed to predict edits that transform an input graph into a target graph. Rather than directly outputting a full graph, the model predicts local edit operations: node insertions, node deletions, and edge insertions or deletions. The architecture computes node representations with message passing over the input graph and then applies an output layer that assigns edit scores to nodes and node pairs. These scores are interpreted as edit actions, enabling the network to model graph-to-graph transformations in a structured and sparse way.

The methodology formulates graph editing as a supervised learning problem. Training examples consist of input graphs together with desired graph transformations, represented through edit scripts or edit indicators derived from source and target graphs. The model is trained to predict which nodes should be deleted, where new nodes should be inserted, and which edges should be added or removed. The paper evaluates the approach on synthetic graph transformation tasks constructed to reflect rule-based edits, and it compares GENs against alternative neural approaches for graph prediction. Reported experiments examine whether the architecture can learn these transformation rules from examples and generalize to unseen graphs.

The paper also studies a variant with restricted edge prediction to improve efficiency on larger graphs. In this formulation, candidate edge edits are limited using node-level signals so that the model does not need to score all possible node pairs. Experiments are presented to assess both predictive performance on the synthetic tasks and the scalability of the method as graph size increases.

flaw_records.description: The reported evaluation setup for the synthetic experiments is not fully specified in the paper, particularly the exact data-generation functions and some details needed to recreate the benchmark tasks. Independent reconstruction required additional interpretation and checking of the released code, meaning the experimental methodology as written is insufficiently precise for direct verification from the paper alone.

The synthetic dataset generation procedures used for evaluation contain implementation or specification problems that required careful re-evaluation by the replication study. Because these generators define the benchmark tasks themselves, errors or ambiguities in them directly affect the validity of the reported experimental evidence.

The paper claims that the proposed architecture scales well to larger graphs, but the replication report explicitly re-examines this point and indicates that the evidence for the scalability claim is limited or contestable. The concern is that the experiments do not fully substantiate broad statements about scaling behavior beyond the tested conditions.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is a methodological mistake to predict edit operations instead of directly generating the full target graph adjacency/structure.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For graph-to-graph transformation tasks, predicting sparse local edits is a principled formulation, not a shortcut. The hypothesis being tested is precisely that many target graphs differ from source graphs by localized rule-based changes, so edit prediction matches the problem structure, reduces output complexity, and preserves correspondence with the input graph. Direct graph generation is only a common default, not an inherently superior formulation here.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Assigning scores to node pairs for edge insertions/deletions is invalid because it treats edges independently and ignores global graph consistency.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Edge decisions based on learned node representations are a standard and justified way to parameterize structured graph edits. In GENs, message passing first encodes graph context into node states, so pairwise edge scores are not actually context-free. For edit prediction tasks, the goal is to identify local modifications conditioned on the whole input graph, and pairwise scoring is a natural output mechanism even if it does not explicitly decode an entire globally constrained graph in one step.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using supervised edit indicators or edit scripts derived from source and target graphs is suspect because the model should be trained only on final target graphs, not on operation-level labels.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Operation-level supervision is appropriate when the intended output is an edit sequence or edit set rather than a standalone graph. Deriving edit labels from aligned source-target graph pairs is exactly how one converts graph transformation into a supervised learning problem. This is especially justified for synthetic rule-based tasks where the transformation semantics are known and the research question is whether the network can recover those edit rules from examples.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Restricting candidate edge predictions using node-level signals is an incorrect approximation that undermines the validity of the model.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This restriction is a defensible efficiency design for larger graphs because exhaustive scoring over all node pairs is quadratic and often unnecessary when only a small subset of nodes are likely to participate in edits. Using node-level signals to gate candidate edge edits is aligned with the sparse-edit assumption underlying the method. It changes the search space for efficiency, but that is a legitimate architectural variant to test, not an automatically flawed choice.

mixed_rationale: The reproduction supports the general implementation and core methodology but documents contestable claims and methodological issues in dataset generation, evaluation, and scalability evidence.

title: Re] Learning to count everything
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a few-shot object counting framework called FamNet that treats counting as a regression problem conditioned on a small number of exemplars from the target category. Given an input image and a few example bounding boxes of the object of interest taken from the same image, the model learns to estimate a density map whose integral gives the final count. This formulation is intended to generalize to novel object categories without category-specific retraining, enabling counting across many visual domains from only a few exemplars at test time.

FamNet is presented together with a test-time adaptation strategy for novel categories. The method uses exemplar patches to condition the counting model and refines prediction for the current category using limited supervision available at inference time. The paper evaluates the effect of the number of exemplars and claims that increasing exemplar count improves accuracy. The approach is compared against prior few-shot counting methods as well as detector-based baselines to show improved counting performance.

To support broad-category evaluation, the paper introduces the FSC-147 dataset, containing more than 6000 images spanning 147 visual categories with annotations suitable for few-shot counting. Experiments are conducted on FSC-147 and also on CARPK, with additional analysis intended to show that the approach remains effective not only on the newly introduced multi-category dataset but also on category-specific counting benchmarks. The main claimed findings are that FamNet outperforms competing few-shot approaches, performs well on category-specific data, and benefits from more exemplars.

flaw_records.description: The procedure for generating ground-truth density maps was not adequately specified and was described incorrectly, while the corresponding code was not released. This made the density-map construction process unverifiable from the paper and required independent reconstruction of missing details, creating ambiguity about a core preprocessing component of the method.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using exemplar bounding boxes from the same test image is data leakage or makes the task invalid because the model gets test-time annotations.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In few-shot class-agnostic counting, the task is explicitly conditioned on a small number of user-provided exemplars that define the object category of interest within the current image. Those boxes are not hidden labels for all instances; they are the allowed input that specifies what to count. This is analogous to prompt-like conditioning rather than illicit access to test targets, and it is central to the paper's intended setting of counting novel categories without category-specific retraining.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Formulating counting as density-map regression instead of detection is misguided, since objects should be detected and then counted.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Density regression is a standard and well-justified alternative in counting, especially when scenes are crowded, objects are small, overlapping, or visually diverse. The paper's goal is category-agnostic few-shot counting across many domains, where detector-style assumptions and category-specific localization heads are often less suitable. Predicting a density map whose integral yields the count is therefore a principled design choice, not a methodological mistake.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Test-time adaptation on the novel category is improper because models should not update at inference time.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In this problem setting, limited supervision is deliberately available at inference through the exemplars, and the purpose of adaptation is to specialize the generic counting model to the current unseen category. This is consistent with few-shot and transductive/meta-test adaptation paradigms, where controlled test-time refinement is part of the method rather than contamination of the evaluation.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating how accuracy changes with the number of exemplars is unnecessary or unfair because the method should be judged with a fixed single exemplar.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The number of exemplars is a core task variable in few-shot counting because exemplars define the target concept and can reduce ambiguity for diverse object appearances. Studying performance as exemplar count increases directly tests the conditioning mechanism and the claimed few-shot behavior. It is therefore a meaningful ablation, not an invalid or suspicious evaluation choice.

mixed_rationale: The main empirical claims were largely supported, but the report explicitly contests the paper's description of ground-truth density-map generation and notes missing implementation details for parts of the methodology.

title: Re] Badder Seeds: Reproducing the Evaluation of Lexical Methods for Bias Measurement
task_prompt:
The original paper studies how lexical seed sets used for NLP bias measurement are constructed and whether their properties affect the behavior of common bias metrics. It first gathers many seed lexicons from prior literature, treating these lexicons as the core objects of analysis. The paper then examines these seed sets both qualitatively, by considering how they were selected and sourced, and quantitatively, by testing how they behave when used in standard bias-measurement procedures.

To run the quantitative analyses, the paper trains word embeddings on multiple corpora and applies established bias measurement methods based on seed words, including approaches associated with WEAT- and PCA-style bias subspaces. Using these embeddings and seed sets, it evaluates whether the induced bias subspaces capture the intended semantic contrast represented by the paired seed terms. It also compares outcomes across alternative seed sets and measurement setups.

A central part of the methodology is to probe the stability of bias measurements under changes to seed-set design. The paper varies factors such as the ordering and pairing of seed terms, similarities among the selected words, and their frequencies in the training data, and then observes how these choices alter the resulting bias subspaces and bias scores. The overall goal is to assess whether commonly reused seed lexicons provide a robust foundation for measuring bias across corpora.

Based on these analyses, the paper argues for more careful validation and justification of seed lexicons before they are reused in bias studies. Its methodology is therefore framed as an evaluation of lexical bias-measurement pipelines, with emphasis on how seed selection decisions influence quantitative conclusions.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The paper is methodologically weak because it reuses seed lexicons from prior literature instead of creating a new, standardized seed set.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The stated goal is to evaluate how existing lexical seed sets behave in bias-measurement pipelines, not to propose a new canonical lexicon. Using many published seed sets as the primary objects of analysis is therefore the correct design: it tests the robustness of what the field actually reuses and directly supports claims about comparability and stability across prior work.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is a flaw to vary seed ordering, pairing structure, word similarity, and word frequency, because those manipulations make the bias evaluation artificial or inconsistent.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: These perturbations are central to the paper's hypothesis about sensitivity of lexical bias measures to seed-set design. In methods like WEAT- or PCA-style subspace construction, seemingly innocuous choices such as pair alignment, token selection, and frequency profile can materially affect the derived direction or score. Stress-testing these factors is a valid robustness analysis, not an invalid departure from a fixed benchmark protocol.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Training word embeddings on multiple corpora introduces unnecessary variation and should have been avoided in favor of a single embedding source.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Because the paper evaluates whether seed lexicons provide a robust foundation for measuring bias across corpora, using multiple corpora is methodologically appropriate. A single corpus could confound corpus-specific lexical statistics with seed-set effects; cross-corpus evaluation is needed to determine whether observed behavior is stable or merely an artifact of one training distribution.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The paper should only report downstream bias scores; checking whether the induced bias subspace actually captures the intended semantic contrast is extraneous or not part of standard evaluation.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For lexical bias methods built from paired seed words, validating that the induced subspace reflects the intended contrast is foundational. If the constructed direction does not encode the target semantic axis, any downstream score is hard to interpret. Examining the subspace itself is therefore an appropriate diagnostic step, especially in a paper whose main question is whether seed-set construction choices undermine measurement validity.

mixed_rationale: The report indicates the paper's main conclusions were largely reproduced, but some methodological details were underspecified and required clarification, making parts of the design contestable from a reproducibility standpoint.

title: Re] Explaining in Style: Training a GAN to explain a classifier in StyleSpace
task_prompt:
The paper proposes StylEx, a method for generating classifier-specific counterfactual explanations by training a StyleGAN2 model together with a target classifier so that the generator’s disentangled StyleSpace captures attributes that are relevant to the classifier’s decisions. Given an input image, the method inverts it into the generator’s latent representation, identifies StyleSpace channels associated with the classifier output, and manipulates those channels to produce edited images that change the classifier prediction while preserving overall realism.

The claimed workflow is domain- and classifier-agnostic. A classifier is first trained for a task of interest, and a StyleGAN2-based generative model is then trained or adapted so that latent style dimensions can be linked to changes in the classifier’s score. Candidate attributes are discovered by analyzing how perturbations in StyleSpace affect the classifier output, and a subset of influential, disentangled attributes is selected as an explanation basis. Counterfactual explanations are generated by editing one or more of these attributes to obtain images that remain visually coherent but cross the decision boundary of the classifier.

The paper evaluates these explanations using criteria such as human interpretability, distinctiveness between discovered attributes, coherence of the corresponding visual edits, and sufficiency for flipping the classifier decision. Experiments are presented on multiple datasets and classifiers, with visual examples showing the top discovered attributes and corresponding counterfactual images. The overall claim is that StylEx identifies meaningful human-interpretable features in StyleSpace and that manipulating these features yields plausible counterfactual explanations of classifier behavior.

flaw_records.description: The paper did not provide enough methodological detail to fully specify the training procedure, network architecture, and hyperparameter configuration needed to reproduce the experiments. The missing information was substantial enough that a full reimplementation and follow-up communication with the first author were required before comparable training runs could be attempted.

The paper presents StylEx explanations as human-interpretable, distinct, coherent, and sufficient to flip classifier decisions, but the reproduced study reports substantially weaker performance for retrained models on human identification of extracted attributes and on the sufficiency measure. This raises a challenge to the breadth of the claimed explanatory quality across settings rather than to a single implementation detail.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Training or adapting the StyleGAN2 generator with respect to a fixed target classifier is methodologically wrong because explanations should be produced with a completely classifier-independent generative model.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This paper’s hypothesis is explicitly classifier-specific explanation: the latent factors should align with features that matter for that classifier’s decision boundary, not with generic semantic factors alone. Coupling or adapting the generator to the classifier is therefore a principled design choice, since a purely classifier-agnostic generator may represent many realistic variations that are irrelevant to the classifier and fail to expose decision-relevant, manipulable attributes.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Searching for explanatory factors in StyleSpace channels rather than in image pixels, class activation maps, or a single compact latent vector is an unjustified and risky choice.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For StyleGAN2, StyleSpace is specifically valued because its channels are more disentangled and locally controllable than raw pixel space or more entangled latent representations. The goal here is not standard attribution but editable counterfactual generation, and channel-wise StyleSpace interventions are well suited to finding factors that can be changed while preserving realism. A reviewer treating pixel-space saliency as the default would miss that the paper is solving a manipulation-and-explanation problem, not merely a localization problem.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using GAN inversion as the starting point for explanations is flawed because inversion introduces reconstruction error, so explanations should instead operate directly on the original image.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For generator-based counterfactuals, inversion is the standard mechanism that maps a real image into the model’s editable latent representation. Some reconstruction loss is an accepted tradeoff for obtaining semantically meaningful latent controls and keeping edits on the generator manifold. Operating directly on the original image would defeat the purpose of using the GAN’s disentangled latent space to produce realistic counterfactual edits.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Selecting only a subset of influential and disentangled StyleSpace attributes as the explanation basis is cherry-picking and therefore invalid; all discovered factors should be retained.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Explanation methods commonly prioritize sparse, high-impact, minimally redundant factors because the aim is interpretable intervention, not exhaustive latent cataloging. In this setting, restricting to influential and relatively disentangled channels is exactly what makes the resulting edits distinct, understandable, and controllable. Keeping all factors would likely increase redundancy and entanglement, making explanations less useful rather than more faithful.

mixed_rationale: The reproduced report supports some claims on released pretrained models but documents that key methodological details were underspecified and that some claimed qualitative and quantitative properties were not matched in retrained settings.

title: Re] Replication Study of DECAF: Generating Fair Synthetic Data Using Causally-Aware Generative Networks
task_prompt:
The original DECAF paper presents a preprocessing approach for fairness in which a generative model is trained on biased tabular data and then used to generate a synthetic dataset intended to be less biased while preserving utility for downstream prediction. The method is based on a causally aware generative adversarial network that incorporates the structure of a directed acyclic graph (DAG), with the goal of removing undesirable bias in the data generation process rather than modifying downstream classifiers directly.

The paper evaluates DECAF in two experimental settings. In the first setting, DECAF is applied to real-world datasets that already contain bias, and the generated synthetic data is used to train downstream models that are then evaluated for both predictive utility and fairness. In the second setting, bias is first synthetically injected into real data and DECAF is then applied to remove that injected bias, again assessing whether the resulting synthetic data supports accurate and fair downstream prediction.

Across these experiments, the paper compares DECAF-generated data against alternative methods using standard data utility and fairness measures on tabular datasets such as Adult. The claimed objective is to show that causally informed synthetic data generation can debias training data in a way that improves fairness of downstream models while retaining useful predictive information, both when evaluated on synthetic data and when models are transferred to real data.

flaw_records.description: The experimental methodology was underspecified to the point that multiple interpretations of both the conceptual setup and the implementation were possible. The report states that reproducing the experiments required rewriting or adding large sections of code and that the provided code was incomplete, making the intended experimental design difficult to determine from the paper and artifacts alone.

The fairness evaluation used in the paper was not reproduced in numerically comparable form: although fairness metrics followed similar trends in the first experiment, their absolute and relative values did not match the reported results. This indicates that the paper's evaluation procedure or metric computation was not specified clearly enough for an independent implementation to recover the same fairness results.

The second experiment, involving synthetic bias injection followed by debiasing, could not be directly reproduced because the experimental setup required considerable changes relative to what was described or provided. The report explicitly notes that these changes make direct comparison difficult, suggesting that the original methodology for this experiment was insufficiently specified or operationalized for faithful replication.
