Some reported findings were described as counterintuitive, including cases where algorithms not designed for debiasing appeared to debias data and sometimes outperform dedicated debiasing methods on the same dataset. This raises concern that the paper may have overinterpreted empirical outcomes or drawn broader methodological conclusions than were well supported by the experimental evidence.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using a preprocessing generative model to improve fairness instead of modifying the downstream classifier is a methodological mistake because fairness should be enforced directly at prediction time.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is a valid design choice for the paper's hypothesis. DECAF is explicitly a data-level debiasing method: it aims to alter the training distribution so that many downstream learners can inherit fairer behavior without changing each classifier separately. In fairness research, preprocessing is a standard paradigm distinct from in-processing and post-processing, and it is appropriate when the claim is about producing reusable fair synthetic data rather than a single fair classifier.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Building the generator around a causal DAG is an unnecessary or suspicious complication; a standard GAN should have been used without structural assumptions.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The causal structure is central to the method's intended mechanism, not an arbitrary embellishment. Because the paper's premise is that undesirable bias is generated through structured causal pathways, incorporating a DAG into the generative model is domain-appropriate: it constrains generation in a way meant to preserve plausible dependencies while intervening on biased mechanisms. A reviewer might prefer simpler black-box generation by default, but that would not test the paper's causal debiasing hypothesis.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating fairness by training downstream models on DECAF-generated synthetic data, including transfer back to real data, is invalid because synthetic data quality should be judged only by likelihood or fidelity metrics on the generated table itself.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For this paper, downstream performance is the relevant target because the claimed utility of the synthetic data is to serve as training data for predictive models under fairness constraints. In synthetic tabular data and fairness work, it is entirely appropriate to assess whether models trained on the synthetic data retain predictive utility and exhibit improved fairness, including when transferred to real test data. Pure generative fidelity metrics would miss the practical objective the method is designed to optimize.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Injecting synthetic bias into real data and then testing whether DECAF removes it is an artificial and therefore invalid experiment that should not be part of the evaluation.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Although artificial, this is a justified stress-test design. Controlled bias injection creates a setting where the source and form of bias are known, allowing the evaluator to test whether the method can remove a targeted biased mechanism under semi-known conditions. This complements evaluation on naturally biased real datasets, where the ground-truth bias process is usually unknown. Such partially synthetic experiments are common when direct causal validation is otherwise impossible.

title: Re] Privacy-preserving collaborative learning with automatic transformation search
task_prompt:
The paper proposes ATSPrivacy, a privacy-preserving collaborative learning framework that applies data augmentations to training images before gradient sharing in order to reduce the risk of gradient-based reconstruction attacks. The central idea is that simple image transformations such as translations, contrast changes, and related augmentations can obfuscate private image content while preserving enough task-relevant information for effective model training.

To choose effective transformations, the method defines a policy as a sequence or combination of augmentations and introduces an automatic transformation search procedure. This search is designed to identify policies that maximize privacy protection while maintaining predictive performance. Because directly evaluating every candidate policy through full model training and reconstruction attacks is expensive, the framework uses search metrics including a privacy score and an accuracy score as proxies to guide policy selection.

The methodology evaluates whether the selected augmentation policies improve privacy under a gradient reconstruction threat model while retaining model utility. The paper claims that the searched policies provide stronger protection than using individual augmentations alone, and that the proposed search metrics correlate with downstream privacy and accuracy outcomes, enabling efficient discovery of effective privacy-preserving augmentation policies for collaborative learning.

flaw_records.description: The claimed privacy protection was too broad because it did not generalize to an attacker with knowledge of the augmentation policy used by the defense. Under this stronger but natural threat model, the protection claim is undermined, indicating that the paper's conclusions about privacy preservation depend on limiting the attacker's knowledge in a way not captured by the broader claim.

The automatic search procedure was presented as finding effective augmentation policies, but the resulting policies were reported to be largely composed of the same augmentations that already perform best individually. This weakens the claim that the search discovers nontrivial or hard-to-predict combinations, since the selected policies appear predictable rather than uniquely emergent from the search process.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Applying label-preserving image augmentations before gradient sharing is fundamentally invalid because augmentations are only for generalization, not for privacy defense.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In this paper's setting, augmentations are intentionally used to alter the information content of the client input before gradients are computed, which directly changes what can be inferred by a gradient-reconstruction attacker. That is a legitimate defense mechanism under the stated threat model as long as utility is retained. Using transformations for both regularization and privacy is unusual but methodologically sound.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using proxy privacy and accuracy scores during policy search is unsound; every candidate policy must be evaluated with full training and actual reconstruction attacks.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For transformation-policy search, exhaustive end-to-end evaluation is typically prohibitively expensive because each candidate would require repeated model training and attack execution. Using carefully designed proxy metrics to rank candidates is a standard practical relaxation in search/AutoML-style methods, especially when the paper explicitly treats them as search guides and later validates them against downstream privacy and utility outcomes.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Searching over sequences or combinations of augmentations is an overcomplicated and unjustified design; each augmentation should be tested only in isolation.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Compositional policies are reasonable here because privacy effects and utility effects of image transformations are not generally additive or independent. A combination such as geometric plus photometric changes can alter reconstruction difficulty differently than either alone while still preserving labels. Exploring policies rather than single operators is therefore a justified design choice for this hypothesis.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Transformations like translation or contrast change should be rejected because they distort the original image and therefore invalidate collaborative learning.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The goal is not faithful image preservation but preservation of task-relevant semantics under a supervised learning objective. Many vision tasks are intentionally trained to be invariant or robust to modest shifts in position, brightness, or contrast, so these transformations can be acceptable perturbations. In a privacy-utility tradeoff framework, allowing such controlled distortions is exactly the point rather than a methodological error.

mixed_rationale: The main results were reportedly reproducible within reasonable error, but the paper's broader protection claims were contestable because the defense did not hold against an attacker who knows the augmentation policy.

title: Re] Robust Counterfactual Explanations on Graph Neural Networks
task_prompt:
The paper proposes RCExplainer, a method for generating counterfactual explanations for graph neural networks in graph classification tasks. In this setting, an explanation is represented as a subset of edges from the input graph. The method is designed so that removing the selected edges leads to a substantial change in the model's prediction, making the explanation counterfactual. The paper positions this as an alternative to prior GNN explanation methods that aim to identify important substructures but do not explicitly optimize for counterfactual behavior or robustness.

The experimental methodology compares RCExplainer against existing GNN explanation baselines on graph classification benchmarks. Explanations are evaluated along three axes: counterfactual quality as measured by fidelity-style metrics based on the effect of removing explanation edges, robustness to noise or perturbations, and computational efficiency. The paper reports training the underlying GNNs and the explainer models, then evaluating the generated edge subsets under these metrics to assess whether RCExplainer produces more effective and stable explanations than prior methods.

The central claim tested by these experiments is that RCExplainer outperforms competing explainers by generating explanations that are more counterfactual, more robust to noisy conditions, and at least as efficient in runtime. The methodology therefore consists of training the explainer, applying it to graph classification models, and benchmarking its explanations against alternative explainers using the same evaluation framework.

flaw_records.description: The report states that the implementation of the evaluation metric was investigated and that concerns were raised about it. This indicates that the metric used to assess explainers may not match the intended or properly defined evaluation procedure, making the reported fidelity or robustness comparisons contestable even though the overall ranking was similar.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using edge removal to define and evaluate explanations is invalid because a good explanation should identify supportive evidence, not delete parts of the graph.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For counterfactual explanation in graph classification, the target object is precisely a set of edges whose removal changes the prediction. This differs from feature-importance or rationale extraction settings. Representing explanations as removable edge subsets is therefore aligned with the paper's hypothesis rather than a methodological mistake.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing RCExplainer to standard GNN explainers is unfair because those baselines were not designed to optimize counterfactual behavior.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's claim is that existing explainers are insufficient for the counterfactual objective and that a method explicitly trained for this goal performs better under that objective. Benchmarking against widely used explanation baselines under a common evaluation protocol is the appropriate way to test that claim, even if the baselines optimize different surrogate notions of explanation.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating explanations by how much the prediction changes after removing the selected edges is circular and therefore invalid.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: That evaluation would be circular only if the paper claimed to measure a different construct. Here the claimed construct is counterfactual quality itself: an explanation is good if intervening on the selected edges alters the model decision. Measuring the effect of removal is thus the direct operationalization of the intended property, not a flawed shortcut.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Including robustness and runtime as evaluation axes is inappropriate because explanation papers should only assess faithfulness or accuracy.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For graph explanations intended for practical use, stability under noise and computational cost are legitimate secondary criteria, especially when the method is explicitly advertised as robust and efficient. Evaluating these dimensions alongside counterfactual quality matches the stated contribution and does not weaken the experimental design.

mixed_rationale: The main empirical claims were largely reproduced, but the report explicitly raises concerns about the correctness of the metric implementation used in the evaluation.

title: Re] Lifting 2D StyleGAN for 3D-Aware Face Generation
task_prompt:
The paper proposes LiftedGAN, a framework for converting a pre-trained 2D StyleGAN2 face generator into a 3D-aware generative model through knowledge distillation. Starting from latent codes of StyleGAN2, the method aims to disentangle the generation process into interpretable components corresponding to texture, shape/depth, viewpoint, lighting, and foreground/background structure. These factors are then combined by a rendering pipeline to synthesize a final 2D face image while exposing explicit controls over pose and illumination.

Architecturally, LiftedGAN is described as a collection of five sub-networks that predict the components needed for 3D-aware rendering: light direction, viewpoint, foreground/background map, depth, and texture. The outputs of these modules are used to form a 3D-consistent intermediate representation, which is rendered back to the image plane. The central claim is that this decomposition allows changing viewpoint and light direction independently while preserving other attributes such as identity, texture, and shape.

The reported experiments evaluate the approach on face-generation benchmarks such as FFHQ and also on AFHQ Cat, comparing both qualitative manipulations and quantitative image-generation quality. The paper claims that the distilled 3D-aware generator achieves image quality competitive with strong face generation baselines, including in terms of FID, while additionally enabling controllable edits of camera pose and lighting. The methodology therefore combines pre-trained 2D GAN priors with disentangled 3D factor prediction and rendering-based synthesis to obtain controllable face generation.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Distilling a 3D-aware generator from a frozen pre-trained 2D StyleGAN2, instead of training a full 3D generative model from scratch with explicit 3D supervision, is fundamentally unsound and cannot yield meaningful 3D control.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is exactly the paper's intended hypothesis and is well motivated for face domains: a strong 2D GAN already captures identity, texture, and appearance statistics, so distillation can transfer that prior into a structured renderer-backed model without requiring expensive 3D data. For faces, weakly/self-supervised 3D factorization from 2D generators is a legitimate design choice because the target is controllable synthesis consistent with the teacher distribution, not guaranteed metric 3D reconstruction.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using separate sub-networks to predict viewpoint, lighting, foreground/background structure, depth, and texture from the same latent code is an over-engineered and likely inconsistent decomposition; a single monolithic network should be used instead.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: A modular factorized design is justified here because the goal is explicit disentanglement and independent control, not just image fidelity. Predicting rendering-relevant factors with separate heads is standard in inverse-graphics-style setups and makes interventions on pose and illumination possible. Sharing a latent source while branching into interpretable components is a reasonable architectural choice when the desired output variables have different semantics and rendering roles.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Rendering the final image from predicted depth/texture/light/viewpoint is an unnecessary bottleneck that will only degrade image quality; the method should generate pixels directly if it wants competitive FID.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The rendering stage is the mechanism that enforces geometric and photometric structure and enables controllable edits. Accepting some architectural constraint in exchange for interpretable pose and lighting manipulation is a domain-appropriate tradeoff in 3D-aware generation. Moreover, the paper explicitly evaluates whether quality remains competitive after imposing this structure, so the use of a rendering bottleneck is not inherently a methodological flaw.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating on FFHQ and also AFHQ Cat is suspicious because a face-specific lifted 3D decomposition should only be considered valid on human faces with stronger geometric assumptions.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Testing on both human faces and cat faces is a defensible robustness check for the lifting/distillation framework, not evidence of misuse. These datasets are commonly used in GAN literature as aligned, object-centric domains with relatively constrained shape variation, making them appropriate for probing whether the factorized 3D-aware design generalizes beyond a single appearance distribution.

title: Re] Learning Unknown from Correlations: Graph Neural Network for
task_prompt:
The paper studies multi-type protein-protein interaction prediction with a focus on interactions between proteins that are both unseen during training, referred to as inter-novel-protein interactions. It argues that standard random train/test splits can obscure this setting and therefore proposes a new evaluation framework designed to better reflect generalization to novel proteins. The evaluation uses multiple train/test construction strategies, including random splits and graph-based splitting schemes such as breadth-first-search (BFS) and depth-first-search (DFS), to control how proteins and their interactions are distributed between training and test sets. Performance is assessed both overall and specifically on inter-novel-protein interactions, with micro-F1 used as a main metric.

To address the prediction task itself, the paper introduces GNN-PPI, a graph neural network method that leverages correlations between proteins through a protein interaction graph. The method is intended to improve prediction quality for interactions involving proteins not observed during training by incorporating relational structure beyond sequence-only modeling. The proposed approach is compared against prior protein-protein interaction methods, including conventional machine learning baselines and deep learning baselines such as PIPR, DNN-PPI, and DPPI.

Experiments are reported on several real-world datasets of different scales, including SHS27k, SHS148k, and STRING. The paper claims that GNN-PPI achieves higher micro-F1 than strong baselines, especially PIPR, across the proposed split strategies, and that its gains are particularly pronounced for inter-novel-protein interaction prediction. It also claims that the proposed evaluation framework provides a more consistent and realistic assessment of cross-dataset generalization and prediction performance for newly discovered proteins.

flaw_records.description: Reported performance can differ substantially across different train-test splits under the newly proposed splitting schemes, to the extent that reproduced results may still fall within standard deviations while showing large absolute discrepancies from the paper's numbers. This indicates sensitivity of the conclusions to the particular split realization and weakens the stability of claims drawn from single or limited split instances.

The proposed evaluation framework based on alternative train/test set construction was treated as a central methodological contribution, but its superiority was itself a point requiring critical inspection rather than something clearly established by the paper. The report specifically frames the need to verify whether the splitting methodology is objectively better and whether it truly mimics the real-world problem the paper aims to address, making this an explicitly contestable aspect of the methodology.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using graph-structured train/test splits such as BFS and DFS instead of only standard random splits is an invalid or biased evaluation choice.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For this paper's hypothesis, the key issue is generalization to proteins unseen during training, especially interactions where both endpoint proteins are novel. Random edge splits can leak protein identity and neighborhood information across train and test, making the task unrealistically easy. BFS/DFS-style graph splits are unusual compared with IID defaults, but they are justified because they explicitly control topological separation and novelty structure, which is exactly what must be stress-tested in PPI prediction.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Reporting a dedicated inter-novel-protein test subset is cherry-picking and the model should be judged only on overall test performance.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's stated target is prediction for interactions involving proteins absent from training, not merely average performance on mixed easy-and-hard cases. Isolating inter-novel-protein interactions is therefore not post hoc slicing but a task-aligned evaluation. Overall scores can be dominated by interactions involving previously seen proteins and obscure failure on the biologically important novel-protein regime, so this stratified reporting is methodologically appropriate.

must_not_claim_details.claim: A GNN that uses the protein interaction graph is inherently leaking labels or unfairly exploiting test-set information, so sequence-only baselines are the only clean comparison.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Using relational structure is the intended modeling assumption of the method, not automatically a leak. In graph learning for biological networks, leveraging correlations across proteins is a principled design choice when the available graph is part of the input domain. The relevant question is whether train/test edges are separated correctly under the split protocol, not whether graph information is used at all. Rejecting the method simply because it is not sequence-only would misunderstand the paper's hypothesis that network context helps infer interactions for unseen proteins.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using micro-F1 as the main metric is inappropriate; only macro-F1 or per-class metrics would be valid for multi-type PPI prediction.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Micro-F1 is a defensible choice in multi-label or multi-type interaction prediction, especially when class frequencies are highly imbalanced and the goal is aggregate decision quality across many interaction instances. It is a common default in this literature and can be more stable for large sparse label spaces. A reviewer might prefer additional metrics, but treating micro-F1 itself as a methodological error would be incorrect.

mixed_rationale: The reproduction largely confirmed the main claims, but it explicitly questioned whether the proposed split-based evaluation is objectively better and noted substantial variability due to train-test split choice.

title: Re] G-Mixup: Graph Data Augmentation for Graph Classification
task_prompt:
The original G-Mixup paper proposes a graph data augmentation method for graph classification based on graphons. The method first groups training graphs by class and estimates a class-conditional graphon for each label from the graphs in that class. These graphons are treated as latent structural summaries of graph distributions. To generate augmented training data, the method interpolates between graphons from different classes using a mixup coefficient, producing mixed graphons and corresponding soft labels. New synthetic graphs are then sampled from these mixed graphons and added to the training set.

The paper evaluates this augmentation strategy in supervised graph classification with graph neural networks. The claimed experimental design compares training with G-Mixup augmentation against training without augmentation and against other graph augmentation baselines. Performance is assessed on standard graph classification benchmarks, and the study also includes analyses of graphon estimation quality and of how interpolated graphons relate to mixtures of graph classes. The paper presents G-Mixup as a simple, model-agnostic augmentation procedure intended to improve graph classification accuracy and robustness across datasets.

flaw_records.description: The comparative evaluation included augmentation baselines whose implementations or tuning were not on equal footing with G-Mixup, so the reported superiority over alternative augmentation strategies is contestable. The replication report frames some baseline comparisons as not being reliably reproducible at the claimed level, indicating that the original comparative setup may have disadvantaged competing methods rather than isolating the effect of the proposed method itself.

Broad claims of superior performance across augmentation strategies and datasets appear stronger than what the reproduced evidence supports. While the core graphon-based augmentation mechanism could be replicated, the report indicates that only some of the original claims were verified and that the paper's general performance conclusions extend beyond what was consistently confirmed under reproduction.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Estimating one graphon per class by pooling graphs with the same label is invalid because graphs in a class are not node-aligned and can have different sizes, so a class-level structural object is meaningless.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This objection misses the role of graphons in exchangeable random graph models: graphons are permutation-invariant latent generators meant precisely to summarize distributions over graphs without requiring node correspondence, and they naturally support variable graph sizes through sampling. For the paper's hypothesis, class-conditional graphon estimation is a principled way to capture shared structural tendencies within a class rather than a mistaken attempt to average adjacency matrices directly.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Interpolating graphons from different classes is an unsound operation because mixing two graph generators creates unrealistic graphs and therefore invalid labels.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The whole method is intentionally a mixup-style augmentation in latent structure space, not a claim that interpolated samples are members of a pure class. The synthetic examples are paired with soft labels, which is exactly the standard justification for interpolation-based augmentation: they regularize the classifier by exposing it to intermediate decision-boundary cases. In this setting, graphon interpolation is the graph-domain analogue of input or latent mixup, so 'not being a pure real-class graph' is expected, not a methodological error.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Sampling new graphs from the mixed graphons is an unreliable extra source of randomness; the method should instead use deterministic mixed graph objects or directly train on the graphons themselves.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The downstream task is graph classification on finite graphs with standard GNNs, so converting mixed graphons back into sampled graphs is the appropriate interface to the learner. Sampling is not gratuitous noise but the mechanism by which a graphon-defined distribution yields concrete training examples of varying size and edge realizations. Using deterministic 'average graphs' would be less faithful to the probabilistic object being modeled and could produce unnatural weighted structures outside the intended task setting.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating graphon estimation quality and checking whether interpolated graphons relate to mixtures of graph classes is a distraction from classification accuracy and suggests the paper is overfitting to an irrelevant surrogate objective.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For this method, those analyses are directly tied to construct validity: the augmentation pipeline depends on class-conditional graphon estimation being meaningful and on interpolated graphons behaving like sensible mixtures in structural space. Such diagnostics are therefore not replacing the end task but testing whether the proposed mechanism is operating as hypothesized. In methods built around an explicit latent structural representation, this kind of intermediate analysis is a justified domain-specific sanity check.

mixed_rationale: The report presents the overall method as largely reproducible, but it documents contestable baseline and evaluation choices that affect the strength of some comparative claims.

title: Re] On the Reproducibility of CartoonX
task_prompt:
The original CartoonX paper proposes an explanation method for image classifiers that identifies image regions important for a model’s prediction while enforcing piece-wise smoothness in the explanation. The method operates in a transform domain and searches for a sparse set of coefficients whose preservation keeps the classifier output close to that of the original image. By optimizing this coefficient mask under a distortion objective and a sparsity-inducing regularization term, the method is intended to produce human-interpretable explanations that highlight coherent cartoon-like structures rather than noisy pixel-level artifacts.

To evaluate the method, the paper compares CartoonX against existing explanation approaches on image classification tasks, focusing on whether the produced explanations are visually piece-wise smooth and semantically meaningful. The paper also assesses faithfulness by measuring how well the classifier output is preserved when only the selected coefficients or corresponding reconstructed image content are retained, and studies the trade-off between distortion and the number of coefficients used. The comparisons are framed against state-of-the-art explainability baselines.

In addition, the paper presents CartoonX as model-agnostic by applying the explanation procedure to different image classifiers rather than tying it to one specific architecture. The methodology therefore combines qualitative inspection of explanations with quantitative distortion-versus-sparsity experiments to argue that CartoonX yields interpretable, faithful, and broadly applicable explanations for image classification models.

flaw_records.description: Some hyperparameter settings and implementation details required to reproduce the reported experiments were not clearly specified in the paper or accompanying code, leaving important parts of the experimental procedure underdetermined and requiring additional clarification from the original authors.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Optimizing explanations in a transform/coefficient domain instead of directly over pixels is an unjustified detour and makes the method less faithful than standard pixel-space saliency methods.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is the core hypothesis of CartoonX, not a methodological mistake. The paper explicitly aims to recover piece-wise smooth, cartoon-like structures, which are more naturally represented sparsely in an appropriate transform domain than in raw pixel space. For this objective, coefficient-domain masking is a principled way to bias explanations toward coherent regions and away from high-frequency pixel noise, while faithfulness is still evaluated through output preservation under reconstruction.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Enforcing sparsity and piece-wise smoothness in the explanation improperly injects a visual prior, so the method is invalid because explanations should be unconstrained and purely data-driven.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For interpretability methods, imposing a structural prior can be a deliberate and defensible design choice when the target notion of explanation is human-interpretable regional structure rather than arbitrary fine-grained attribution. CartoonX is explicitly designed to trade off fidelity against compactness and smoothness, and then assess that trade-off quantitatively. A reviewer should not treat the existence of this prior itself as an error; it is aligned with the paper's stated explanatory goal.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using distortion-versus-sparsity curves and preservation of classifier output under retained coefficients is the wrong evaluation, because explanations should only be judged by overlap with human-labeled semantic regions or insertion/deletion metrics.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For a method defined as finding a sparse subset of transform coefficients whose preservation keeps the model prediction close to the original, distortion under coefficient retention is a directly matched faithfulness criterion. It tests exactly whether the selected explanation components suffice for the model's decision. While other metrics can be useful, criticizing this task-aligned evaluation as inherently invalid would be incorrect.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Calling the method model-agnostic is misleading unless it is demonstrated on many architectures or uses no model outputs beyond a single specific network setup.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Model-agnostic in explainability usually means the procedure is not tied to a particular internal architecture, gradient pathway, or layer structure, and can be applied to different classifiers through their input-output behavior. The task prompt states that the method is applied across different image classifiers rather than built for one architecture. A reviewer should not reject the model-agnostic claim merely because the evaluation is not exhaustive over every possible architecture.

mixed_rationale: The replication largely supports the paper’s main claims, but it also notes that some implementation details and hyperparameter settings needed for reproduction were insufficiently specified.

title: Re] Reproducibility Study of “Label-Free Explainability for Unsupervised Models”
task_prompt:
The paper proposes a label-free post-hoc explainability framework for unsupervised models. Its methodology evaluates whether explanations can be produced without access to class labels by assigning importance both to input features and to training examples. The approach is tested across several unsupervised learning settings, including disentangled variational autoencoders and pretext-task-based representation learning, with the goal of identifying which parts of an input and which training instances are most responsible for a model’s behavior on a given test example.

For feature importance, the claimed methodology computes saliency-style importance scores over input dimensions and evaluates whether the highest-scoring features correspond to salient generative factors or informative regions of the input. For example importance, the method assigns relevance scores to training samples for a target test point, aiming to recover training examples that best explain the model’s response to that test sample. These analyses are performed in a label-free setting, so the explanations are derived from the model and data structure rather than supervised annotations.

The paper further studies interpretability in disentangled VAEs, claiming that saliency maps are harder to interpret in that setting, and compares representations learned from distinct self-supervised or pretext tasks to test whether explanations transfer across representations. The experimental design therefore centers on four main claims: feature importance identifies salient input features, example importance identifies salient training instances for a test example, saliency-map interpretability is challenging for disentangled VAEs, and representations learned from different pretext tasks are not interchangeable.

flaw_records.description: The claim that the paper’s conclusions generalize beyond the original experimental settings is contestable. Additional experiments indicated that at least one main claim did not extend robustly to datasets outside those originally used, suggesting that the paper’s framing may overstate the breadth of applicability of its methodology.

One experimental claim could not be reproduced on the graph dataset, and the exact cause was not isolated. The report indicates that the original methodology was difficult to extrapolate to other datasets and that one experiment failed to reproduce despite exploratory checks, leaving a dataset-specific robustness issue in the claimed setup.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The evaluation is invalid because it does not use class labels or supervised ground truth to verify explanations.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper’s central hypothesis is specifically label-free explainability for unsupervised models, so avoiding labels is not a methodological omission but the object of study. In this setting, explanations are meant to arise from the learned structure of the model and data, and using class labels as the primary explanatory target would change the problem into a supervised one.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Assigning importance to training examples for a test point is inappropriate or ad hoc in unsupervised learning because there is no prediction label to explain.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Example-based explanation is still meaningful for unsupervised models because these models produce test-point-specific behavior through their learned representation, reconstruction, similarity structure, or latent response. Identifying influential training samples is a legitimate way to probe what parts of the training distribution the model is relying on, even when no class prediction is present.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using saliency-style feature importance on disentangled VAEs is the wrong methodology, since saliency maps are expected to be less interpretable there.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: That reduced interpretability is itself one of the claims being investigated, not evidence of a design error. Testing saliency in disentangled VAEs is justified because the paper is comparing how explanation behavior changes across unsupervised model families, including cases where standard visual saliency may become harder to interpret due to latent-factor structure.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing explanations across representations learned from different self-supervised or pretext tasks is unfair because such representations need not be interchangeable.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The non-interchangeability of representations is precisely the hypothesis under examination. It is a sound design choice to compare explanations across distinct pretext-task-trained encoders in order to test whether explanation methods are representation-dependent rather than universally transferable.

mixed_rationale: Most core claims were reproduced, but the report states that one claim was not generalizable and another was not reproducible on the graph dataset.

title: Re] FOCUS: Flexible Optimizable Counterfactual Explanations for Tree Ensembles
task_prompt:
The paper proposes FOCUS, a method for generating counterfactual explanations for tree-ensemble classifiers. Given an input instance and a desired target prediction, the method searches for a modified version of the instance that changes the model output while staying close to the original input under a chosen distance function. The approach is designed to be flexible, allowing different optimization objectives and constraints to be incorporated, and is intended to work across tree-based models such as random forests and boosted trees.

Methodologically, the paper formulates counterfactual generation as an optimization problem over the feature space induced by the tree ensemble. It uses the structure of decision trees to identify feasible regions associated with alternative predictions, and then optimizes within those regions to obtain counterfactuals that satisfy the target class while minimizing distance from the original instance. The method is presented as capable of always returning a valid counterfactual explanation for instances in the evaluated datasets.

For evaluation, the paper compares FOCUS against an existing counterfactual explanation method for tree-based models. Experiments are conducted on several datasets and tree-based classifiers, and performance is assessed primarily by whether a valid counterfactual can be found for each instance and by the mean distance between the original input and the generated counterfactual. The paper’s central claim is that FOCUS can generate counterfactual explanations for all tested instances and often finds explanations that are closer to the original input than the baseline method.

flaw_records.description: The experimental evidence does not fully support the broad claim that FOCUS finds counterfactual explanations that are closer to the original input than the existing method across tree-based algorithms. Replication of the original setup and additional re-implementation experiments showed only partial superiority, with some models matching or failing to improve on the baseline in mean distance even though valid counterfactuals were generally produced.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Formulating counterfactual search over the tree-induced feature-space regions, rather than using a smooth gradient-based optimization in the original input space, is an invalid or overly restrictive design choice.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For tree ensembles, the prediction function is piecewise constant and non-differentiable, so standard gradient-based counterfactual optimization is not a natural default. Exploiting the partition of feature space created by tree thresholds is a domain-appropriate way to characterize feasible regions that guarantee specific model outputs. This is not an arbitrary restriction but a method aligned with the structure of the model class being explained.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating FOCUS primarily by counterfactual validity and mean distance is methodologically inadequate because it omits broader explanation-quality metrics.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For counterfactual explanations, validity and proximity are core first-order objectives: the explanation must achieve the requested target prediction and should remain close to the original instance under the chosen distance. Especially in a reproduction or method comparison focused on whether the algorithm can find valid counterfactuals and how close they are, these are standard and directly relevant metrics. While additional criteria can be useful, their omission does not by itself make this evaluation design unsound.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing only against an existing counterfactual method specialized for tree-based models is too narrow and indicates an unfair or weak experimental design.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: A focused comparison to a directly relevant prior method for the same model family is a sensible design choice when the paper's contribution is a new method for tree ensembles. General-purpose baselines may rely on assumptions ill-suited to trees or may be incomparable in feasibility guarantees and search space handling. Using a strong in-domain baseline is therefore justified rather than inherently problematic.

mixed_rationale: The replication supports the claim that FOCUS can generate counterfactuals broadly, but the reported superiority in mean distance over the baseline is only partially sustained.

title: Re] Fairness Guarantees under Demographic Shift
task_prompt:
The original paper proposes a family of algorithms called Shifty for learning classifiers that satisfy fairness constraints with high confidence even when the demographic composition of the deployment population may differ from that of the training population. The methodology assumes a bounded demographic shift, represented through intervals that constrain how much the group proportions can change between training and deployment. Within this uncertainty set, the method optimizes predictive performance while enforcing fairness guarantees that are intended to hold under worst-case shifts.

The approach combines standard supervised learning with uncertainty-aware fairness constraint handling. The paper claims that, given enough training data, Shifty can produce models whose fairness properties remain valid after deployment despite demographic shift, and can do so without sacrificing model accuracy. The experimental evaluation is designed to compare Shifty against relevant alternatives in settings where the deployment distribution differs from the observed training distribution, assessing whether fairness constraints are maintained and how predictive performance changes.

The empirical study uses the model architectures, optimization procedures, and hyperparameter settings specified by the paper to evaluate Shifty under demographic shift scenarios. The main reported outcomes are fairness satisfaction under shifted populations, confidence in those guarantees, and classification accuracy, with the goal of showing that the proposed method provides reliable fairness guarantees while retaining competitive performance.

flaw_records.description: The paper's methodology is presented as providing high-confidence fairness guarantees under demographic shift without loss in model performance given sufficient training data, but the reported replication evidence only supported the fairness-guarantee portion and did not verify the no-loss-of-accuracy claim. This makes the performance claim broader than what the reproduced experimental evidence could substantiate.

Certain hyperparameter and model specifications differed between the written paper and the released implementation, creating ambiguity about which exact experimental configuration constituted the claimed method. This inconsistency complicates independent verification because the intended setup cannot be read unambiguously from the paper alone.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is a methodological error to model deployment shift only through bounded changes in demographic group proportions rather than full covariate shift or arbitrary distribution shift.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's hypothesis is specifically about demographic shift, not unconstrained distribution shift. Restricting the uncertainty set to changes in group prevalence is a deliberate problem formulation that makes worst-case fairness guarantees meaningful and tractable. A reviewer may prefer broader shift models by default, but using a narrower, structured shift model is justified because the claimed guarantee is targeted to exactly that kind of shift.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Evaluating fairness by checking worst-case satisfaction over an uncertainty set is overly pessimistic and therefore a flawed experimental design; fairness should be measured only on the observed test distribution.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Worst-case evaluation is the point of the method: the claim is that fairness constraints remain valid after deployment even when demographic composition changes within specified bounds. Measuring only on the observed test mix would fail to test the stated guarantee. What looks unusually conservative is actually aligned with the robustness objective being studied.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using interval constraints on group proportions is an arbitrary or ad hoc way to represent deployment uncertainty and therefore undermines the validity of the fairness guarantee.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Interval-bounded group proportions are a standard robust-optimization style way to encode partial knowledge about deployment composition. They allow the method to state guarantees conditional on an explicit uncertainty set, which is much more principled than assuming the deployment mix is known exactly. The guarantee is not unconditional; it is intentionally conditioned on the shift lying within those bounds.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The experiments are invalid because they intentionally test on populations with a different demographic composition from training, violating the usual train/test matching assumption.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For ordinary iid generalization this would be concerning, but here mismatch between training and deployment composition is the central phenomenon under study. Constructing shifted evaluation populations is necessary to test whether the proposed fairness guarantees survive demographic shift. A pattern-matching objection based on iid norms would miss that this is a stress test by design.

mixed_rationale: The replication supports the fairness-guarantee claim under demographic shift but indicates that the no-loss-of-accuracy claim is not verified and that some methodological specifications were inconsistent between paper and code.

title: Re] DialSummEval - Evaluation of automatic summarization evaluation metrics
task_prompt:
The original paper studies how automatic evaluation metrics behave for dialogue summarization. It compares metric scores against human judgments on summaries produced for dialogue datasets, with the goal of identifying which metrics align best with human assessment and whether findings from conventional summarization transfer to the dialogue setting.

The methodology centers on collecting human annotations for summaries generated by multiple dialogue summarization systems, together with reference summaries. Human judges rate summaries along several quality dimensions such as coherence, consistency, fluency, and relevance, following annotation guidelines defined by the paper. These human scores are then aggregated and used as the benchmark against which automatic metrics are evaluated.

A broad set of automatic summarization evaluation metrics is computed over the candidate summaries and references. The paper then analyzes correlations between metric scores and human judgments, both overall and by quality dimension, to determine which metrics are effective for dialogue summarization evaluation. In addition, the study compares different summarization models to examine how dialogue-specific models perform across the human-evaluated dimensions.

flaw_records.description: The methodological description of how raw human annotations were post-processed into final analysis inputs was unclear, leaving important steps in the annotation handling pipeline insufficiently specified for independent verification or exact reimplementation.

The code used to calculate inter-annotator agreement was missing, which made the agreement analysis insufficiently transparent and prevented direct confirmation that the reported reliability statistics were produced exactly as described.

The annotation guidelines contained deficiencies that left some labeling decisions under-specified, creating room for ambiguity in how annotators should judge summary quality dimensions and making exact procedural reproduction more difficult.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using reference-based automatic metrics to evaluate dialogue summaries is methodologically invalid because dialogue summarization is too open-ended for reference comparison.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's central research question is precisely whether standard automatic metrics, many of which are reference-based, transfer to dialogue summarization. Computing such metrics and comparing them against human judgments is therefore not an invalid assumption but the object of study. Even if dialogue summarization is more open-ended than news summarization, testing metric-human alignment in this harder setting is a justified design choice.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Human evaluation should use only one overall quality score; separating coherence, consistency, fluency, and relevance is an unnecessary and flawed decomposition.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For dialogue summarization, different failure modes can dissociate more strongly than in conventional summarization, such as fluent but factually inconsistent summaries or relevant but poorly structured ones. Analyzing metric correlation by dimension is therefore a sound design choice because it reveals whether a metric tracks specific aspects of quality rather than only a coarse global impression.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing metrics against aggregated human judgments is wrong because aggregation hides annotator variation and should be avoided entirely.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: When the goal is to benchmark automatic metrics against human assessment, using aggregated human scores as the comparison target is standard and justified: it reduces individual annotator noise and approximates consensus quality judgments better than single ratings. Reviewer concern would be reasonable only if aggregation were mishandled or undocumented, but aggregation itself is not a methodological flaw.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Including multiple summarization systems, especially dialogue-specific and non-dialogue-specific models, contaminates the metric analysis because metrics should be tested on outputs from a single model family.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Evaluating metric behavior across outputs from multiple systems is a strength, not a weakness, because meaningful correlation analysis requires variation in summary quality and error types. In dialogue summarization, comparing different model classes is especially justified since the paper also asks whether conclusions from conventional summarization transfer to dialogue-focused systems and datasets.

mixed_rationale: The main methodology and claims were largely reproduced, but the report identifies some unclear or missing methodological details around annotation post-processing and inter-annotator agreement computation.

title: Re] On the Reproducibility of \"FairCal: Fairness Calibration for Face Verification\
task_prompt:
The original paper proposes FairCal, a post-hoc calibration method for face verification systems designed to improve both probability calibration and fairness across sensitive groups. The setting is pairwise face verification: a pretrained face recognition model produces similarity scores or embeddings for image pairs, and the goal is to transform these scores into calibrated match probabilities. The method specifically considers ethnicity as a sensitive attribute and evaluates whether calibration can reduce disparities such as unequal false positive rates across groups while maintaining strong overall verification performance.

FairCal operates by partitioning the embedding or score space using an unsupervised clustering procedure, described as K-means clustering, and then learning calibration functions within these discovered regions rather than using a single global calibrator. The intent is to capture local structure in the verification space without explicitly calibrating separately for protected groups. The paper compares FairCal against existing calibration approaches and presents it as a fairness-aware calibration strategy that can produce well-calibrated probabilities while preserving or improving face verification accuracy.

Experiments are conducted on the Balanced Faces in the Wild (BFW) and Racial Faces in the Wild (RFW) datasets using embeddings from pretrained face recognition models. The evaluation focuses on three claimed outcomes: strong global accuracy, improved calibration quality of predicted probabilities, and greater predictive equality measured through false positive rate parity across sensitive attributes. The paper reports state-of-the-art or competitive results on these criteria and argues that unsupervised partition-based calibration is an effective approach for fair face verification.

flaw_records.description: The experimental methodology omitted necessary details about image preprocessing and did not provide the assumed embedding files used for the reported experiments on BFW and RFW. Because the calibration pipeline depends on pretrained face embeddings, the absence of a precise preprocessing specification and the missing intermediate artifacts make the exact experimental setup underdetermined and prevent exact reconstruction of the reported results.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using unsupervised K-means partitions of the embedding/score space before calibration is an invalid or arbitrary choice because calibration should be learned globally with a single mapping.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For pairwise face verification, score reliability can vary across different regions of the representation space, so a single global calibrator can be too coarse. Region-wise post-hoc calibration is a defensible way to capture local score-to-probability behavior while still remaining post-hoc and model-agnostic. The clustering is not meant to predict labels itself, but to identify subspaces where calibration differs.

must_not_claim_details.claim: A fairness method that cares about ethnicity must explicitly fit separate calibrators for each protected group; avoiding direct group-conditional calibration is a methodological mistake.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper's hypothesis is precisely that fairness gains can come from partition-based calibration without hard-coding protected-group-specific mappings. That is a legitimate design choice: it can reduce disparities by correcting local miscalibration patterns while avoiding direct dependence on sensitive attributes at inference or reducing the risk of overfitting to coarse group labels. A reviewer may prefer group-wise calibration by default, but not using it is not inherently incorrect.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Improving predictive equality via false positive rate parity should not be evaluated in a calibration paper, because calibration and threshold-based fairness are unrelated objectives.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: In face verification, deployed decisions are threshold-based, and unequal false positive rates across demographic groups are a central fairness concern. It is therefore appropriate to test whether better calibrated match probabilities also improve downstream predictive equality. The paper does not confuse the metrics; it evaluates calibration quality and FPR parity as separate but practically linked outcomes.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Working in the pairwise verification setting with pretrained embeddings and only post-hoc score transformation is too weak or inappropriate; fairness should instead be addressed by retraining the face model end-to-end.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Post-hoc calibration on pretrained verification systems is a realistic and important setting because many face recognizers are deployed as fixed black-box models whose embeddings or similarity scores are available but cannot be retrained. Evaluating fairness and calibration improvements under this constraint is methodologically sound and aligned with the paper's stated goal of a model-agnostic calibration layer.

mixed_rationale: The report indicates the core methodology was largely reproducible in trend, but some claimed experimental details were insufficiently specified and exact results could not be matched due to undocumented preprocessing and missing intermediate artifacts.

title: Re] Reproducibility Study: Label-Free Explainability for
task_prompt:
The original paper proposes a label-free explainability framework for unsupervised models by adapting feature-importance and example-importance methods so they can be applied without class labels. The methodology treats self-supervised or unsupervised representations as the object to be explained, and studies whether explanations can recover meaningful data attributes and influential training examples from learned latent spaces. The paper evaluates this idea on multiple datasets and model settings, including image benchmarks and representation-learning architectures trained with different pretext tasks.

For feature importance, the paper extends linear explanation methods to the unsupervised setting and uses them to identify which input attributes most strongly affect latent representations. For example importance, it adapts influence-style methods to estimate which training samples are most responsible for a representation or prediction in the unsupervised regime. The reported experiments compare explanations across settings such as different datasets and representation-learning objectives, with visualizations and quantitative summaries used to assess whether the methods surface semantically meaningful features and influential examples.

The paper also studies how representation learning choices affect explanations. In particular, it compares representations produced by different pretext tasks to test whether they are interchangeable, and it analyzes saliency maps in relation to latent disentanglement. To do so, it computes correlations between interpretability-related measures and disentanglement of latent units, using autoencoder-based evaluation and Pearson correlation analyses to support claims about whether more disentangled latents yield more interpretable saliency maps.

must_not_claim_details.claim: It is invalid to apply feature-importance methods like linear explanations without class labels, because explanations must be defined relative to a supervised prediction target.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The paper’s core hypothesis is that the learned representation itself can serve as the object of explanation in unsupervised or self-supervised settings. Explaining variation in latent coordinates or representation outputs is a legitimate target when labels do not exist, and adapting feature-importance methods to latent-space behavior is exactly the point of a label-free explainability framework.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using influence-style example-importance methods in an unsupervised regime is inherently incorrect, since influence functions only make sense for labeled training losses.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Influence-style methods fundamentally track how training samples affect a model’s learned outputs through the training objective; they do not require semantic class labels in principle. If the model is trained with a self-supervised or unsupervised objective, estimating which training examples most affect a learned representation or downstream unsupervised prediction is a reasonable domain-specific extension rather than a methodological mistake.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Comparing explanations across different pretext tasks is a flawed experimental design because representations from different self-supervised objectives are not expected to be directly interchangeable.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Testing whether different pretext-task representations are interchangeable is itself a substantive research question, not an invalid assumption. The methodology explicitly studies how representation-learning choices alter explanations, so cross-objective comparison is justified as a way to probe sensitivity of explainability to the upstream self-supervised objective.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Relating saliency-map interpretability to latent disentanglement using Pearson correlations and autoencoder-based disentanglement measures is too indirect to be a valid analysis.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: This is an associational analysis, not a claim of causal identification, and correlation-based evaluation is a standard way to test whether interpretability metrics co-vary with disentanglement measures. In a representation-learning study, using established disentanglement evaluations together with Pearson correlation to assess whether more disentangled latents align with more interpretable saliency is a defensible design choice.
